title: Use metacognition and error logs to choose the next learning action

objectives.statement: Classify a bounded learning error by locating the earliest unsupported operation and justify an uncertainty or discriminating probe.
Calculate and interpret a signed confidence–performance gap without treating one task result as a stable learner trait.
Choose a next learning action that targets the diagnosed operation and specify a fresh probe that can confirm or revise the diagnosis.

scenes.title: Turn an error into a next decision
scenes.contentHtml:
Turn an error into a next decision

Two learners submit the same wrong answer. One interpreted the prompt accurately and remembered the needed rules but selected the wrong model. The other selected the right model and made an execution error. The score is the same, but the most useful next action is not.

This lesson turns a study result into a bounded decision. Metacognitive monitoring (term-metacognitive-monitoring) asks what the current evidence supports. Metacognitive control (term-metacognitive-control) uses that judgment to choose what to do next. You will connect them with a small error log that records evidence, a provisional error hypothesis, confidence calibration, a targeted action, and a fresh probe.

By the end, you will be able to:

  1. locate the earliest unsupported operation in a response;
  2. compare confidence with scored performance on the same task; and
  3. choose a repair and a new probe that could confirm or revise the diagnosis.

The lesson does not teach a universal psychological taxonomy, diagnose a person, prove that logging causes learning, or establish permanent mastery. It does not support admissions, clinical, credit, or professional decisions. Its categories are a useful study-decision model whose adequacy must be tested against new performance.

Route through the lesson

First retrieve the durable-performance loop from topic-orientation-mastery-and-retrieval. Then build the monitoring–control model, learn the calibration calculation, inspect a complete worked record, repair a score-only response, practise with fading support, and redesign a weekly review system. The final assessment uses new fictional evidence.

Accessibility and alternatives

Every essential relation appears in source-order prose. Tables can be read one row at a time or rewritten with one field per line. No task depends on color, position, sound, motion, hover, dragging, handwriting, or speed. Responses may be typed, spoken to text, dictated to a scribe, or supplied in another structured text form while preserving the same reasoning. Use fictional or learner-owned records; do not enter names, grades linked to identity, health information, disability details, or another person's private data.

Source Note
claims claim-monitoring-controlsources source-debruin-monitoring-control

The scholarly review supports the bounded monitoring–control distinction used here. The lesson's fictional cases, operation chain, and log fields are original instructional designs, not a reproduced instrument.

scenes.title: Retrieve the durable-performance loop
scenes.contentHtml:
Retrieve the durable-performance loop

Attempt this low-stakes check before reopening the prerequisite lesson. The purpose is to reveal which part of the prior model is available now.

Check
id check-prerequisite-loop

Without notes, write an ordered study loop that includes:

  1. the performance to be produced;
  2. what happens before feedback;
  3. how an error changes the next action; and
  4. what later evidence is needed before a durable-performance judgment.

Then answer: where would a confidence judgment enter the loop, and why can it not replace the performance record?

Commit your answer before reading the criterion below.

Criterion and route

A usable reconstruction names a target performance, an unaided retrieval attempt, comparison with an answer model or rubric, a repair selected from the error, and another retrieval after a delay or bounded variation. Confidence can be recorded beside the attempt, but it becomes informative only when compared with the observed result.

If your sequence stops at rereading, use this self-contained recovery before continuing:

  1. Target: write one exact performance that a new task will require.
  2. Retrieve: attempt one small case without notes and commit the response before feedback.
  3. Compare: use the supplied answer model or rubric to mark the earliest unsupported step.
  4. Repair: practise only that step with enough support to make the decision visible.
  5. Retrieve later: schedule a different case after a delay or bounded change in wording, values, or representation, then compare again.

If you included an attempt and feedback but not a later probe, add step 5 now. If you cannot yet produce the target, unaided attempt, comparison, repair, and later probe even with this scaffold, pause this lesson and practise the five-step loop on two fictional tasks. If the loop is available but confidence feels separate, continue: this lesson will connect the two records explicitly.

The graph places topic-orientation-metacognition-and-error-log after topic-orientation-mastery-and-retrieval and before topic-orientation-study-plan. This means the error record consumes performance evidence from the prior outcome and supplies decisions to the later plan; it does not mean every learner must use one fixed schedule.

Source Note
claims claim-retrieval-cues, claim-curriculum-sequencesources source-chen-retrieval-judgments, source-authoring-evidence, source-premed-graph

The research sources support retrieval before judgment as a useful monitoring route within bounded tasks. The graph source establishes the curriculum links. The exact diagnostic prompt and recovery routes are original.

scenes.title: Monitor, diagnose, control, and probe
scenes.contentHtml:
Monitor, diagnose, control, and probe

An error log is useful only when it closes a decision loop. A list of wrong answers records history; it does not yet explain what failed or what evidence should come next.

The four-part decision loop

  1. Monitor: preserve the attempt before feedback, its conditions, intermediate work, rubric performance, and confidence.
  2. Diagnose: locate the earliest operation for which the record lacks adequate evidence.
  3. Control: choose the smallest learning action that directly exercises that operation.
  4. Probe: attempt a fresh, bounded case after repair; revise the diagnosis and confidence from the result.

Monitoring and control are connected because the chosen action should respond to the monitored evidence. The fresh probe matters because the diagnosis is a hypothesis. If a targeted selection contrast produces correct model choice but execution still fails, the new evidence moves the earliest unsupported link.

Locate the first broken link

Use this ordered representation:

interpret → retrieve → select → execute → verify

  • Interpret: identify what the task, representation, conditions, and output require.
  • Retrieve: bring the relevant concept, rule, relation, or procedure to mind before feedback.
  • Select: choose which available model or procedure fits the conditions.
  • Execute: apply the selected model accurately and completely.
  • Verify: check the result against units, boundary conditions, an alternative representation, or another task-specific criterion.

The instructional job of this chain is localization. Start at interpretation and move forward. The first broken link (term-first-broken-link) is the earliest operation whose required evidence is absent or contradicted. Later failures may be consequences. These categories are not claimed to be exhaustive psychological causes; they are an author-created procedure for choosing a discriminating next probe.

Add calibration without replacing performance

After committing the attempt but before feedback, predict the percentage of points the response will earn on the declared rubric. Compare that prediction with the percentage actually earned on the same rubric. This matched comparison is calibration (term-calibration). High predicted performance is not evidence of accuracy by itself, and a low prediction does not erase a strong response. The pair—predicted and observed rubric performance—reveals a gap that can be updated after another task.

Do not subtract unlike quantities. A 70% probability that an entire answer is correct and a 40% partial-credit rubric score answer different questions. Either predict the rubric percentage directly or report the two measures separately.

Retrieval supplies observable performance cues that rereading alone cannot. In the cited bounded laboratory tasks, the reported advantage concerned the across-item rank correspondence between judgments and later accuracy after retrieval versus restudy. That repeated-item correspondence is mathematically distinct from this lesson's signed gap for one task: either measure can look favourable while the other does not. The finding therefore supports retrieval performance as one useful cue; it does not make retrieval the only useful cue, validate this lesson's gap, or guarantee accurate judgment in every domain.

Minimal error-log row

Record these fields in order:

Field Question
Task and conditions What performance was required, with what support, delay, and representation?
Attempt evidence What did the learner actually produce before feedback?
First broken link Which earliest operation is unsupported, and what alternative remains possible?
Calibration What was the predicted rubric percentage, what percentage was earned, and what was their signed difference?
Next action Which activity directly exercises the proposed broken link?
Fresh probe What new case and timing could confirm or revise the diagnosis?

In a linear format, write each field as a heading followed by one or two sentences. The table carries no information that is absent from the ordered questions.

Source Note
claims claim-monitoring-control, claim-calibration-definition, claim-retrieval-cues, claim-error-log-loop, claim-operational-categoriessources source-debruin-monitoring-control, source-chen-retrieval-judgments, source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard

The sources support monitoring linked to control, calibration against performance, retrieval-based cues, and feedback that produces a next action. The five-link chain and six log fields are an explicitly bounded author synthesis pending review.

scenes.title: Put confidence and performance on one scale
scenes.contentHtml:
Put confidence and performance on one scale

Definition
id definition-calibration-gap

For one task, let confidence CC be the learner's predicted percentage of points on a declared rubric and let performance PP be the percentage actually earned on that same rubric. The signed calibration gap is G=C−PG=C-P, measured in percentage points.

  • G>0G>0 means confidence exceeded performance on that task.
  • G<0G<0 means confidence was lower than performance on that task.
  • G=0G=0 means the two values matched; it does not prove that either measure is perfect.

Read the expression as “predicted rubric percentage minus observed rubric percentage.” A prediction of 85% with rubric performance of 55% gives a gap of positive 30 percentage points. A prediction of 45% with performance of 80% gives negative 35 percentage points.

The sign answers direction; the magnitude answers distance on the matched scoring scale. Neither answers why the gap occurred. A learner may predict the rubric inconsistently, a rubric may omit an important operation, or a single item may be noisy. Therefore record the task, scoring rule, support, and timing beside the numbers and look for a pattern across comparable probes. If confidence instead means the probability that the whole response is correct, do not subtract it from a partial-credit score; report the measures separately or use an all-or-none outcome.

Calibrate a judgment, not a person

Use task-bounded language:

  • “Predicted rubric performance exceeded observed performance on this selection task by 30 points.”
  • “Predicted rubric performance was lower than observed performance on two varied retrieval tasks.”

Avoid trait labels such as “an overconfident learner.” One result does not establish a stable characteristic. Feedback can ask the learner to point to the evidence used for the confidence judgment and then compare a later judgment with a later result.

Categories remain provisional

The five operation categories—interpret, retrieve, select, execute, verify—organize evidence for action. They do not become more certain because a number was calculated. If intermediate work is missing, write “unresolved between selection and execution” and choose a probe that makes the plan visible.

One controlled study found that adaptive feedback about metacognitive judgments improved calibration on the trained perceptual task relative to performance-only feedback and generalized to an untrained recognition-memory task. The study aggregated repeated trial-level probability judgments with a quadratic score equal to one minus the Brier score and also analysed signal-detection measures; it did not use this lesson's one-task signed rubric-percentage gap. The result therefore supports the general claim that confidence–performance correspondence can be revised with feedback, not the behaviour or validity of this lesson's GG. The small laboratory study also does not prove general transfer to every Premed task or validate this lesson's log.

Source Note
claims claim-calibration-definition, claim-operational-categories, claim-calibration-feedbacksources source-debruin-monitoring-control, source-content-standard, source-carpenter-calibration-training

The broad confidence–performance relation is source grounded. The lesson's signed-gap convention is explicitly separated from the study's repeated-trial quadratic and signal-detection measures; the examples and task-bounded language are original here.

scenes.title: Trace a high-confidence wrong answer
scenes.contentHtml:
Trace a high-confidence wrong answer

Worked Example
id worked-example-model-selection

Task. A fictional rule-selection exercise supplies two models:

  • Model A applies only when condition Alpha is present.
  • Model B applies only when condition Beta is present.

The prompt states that Beta is present and asks the learner to choose a model, carry out its two supplied steps, and state a conclusion. The rubric assigns 25 points each for interpreting the condition, retrieving both model rules, selecting the applicable model, and correctly executing the applicable model's steps. The execution criterion is contingent on applicability: accurately carrying out an inapplicable model earns zero execution points.

Observed record. Before feedback, Kai correctly restates that Beta is present, writes both model rules accurately, selects Model A, executes Model A's supplied steps without a procedural slip, and gives the wrong conclusion. After committing the response, Kai predicts earning 90% of the declared rubric points. The observed rubric performance is 50%: interpretation earns 25 points and retrieval earns 25 points; selection earns 0 because Model A is inapplicable, and execution earns 0 under the declared contingent rule.

Unknown. Which operation first failed, how is confidence calibrated on this attempt, and what should happen next?

Model selection. Use the monitor → diagnose → control → probe loop because the task supplies an observable response chain, scored performance, and confidence. Locate the earliest broken link before choosing an activity.

Plan. Check interpretation, retrieval, selection, execution, and verification in that order. Compute confidence minus performance. Choose an action that discriminates the proposed cause, then specify a new probe.

Execution.

  1. Interpretation is supported: Kai correctly records Beta.
  2. Retrieval is supported: both conditional rules are stated accurately.
  3. Selection is contradicted: Beta calls for Model B, but Kai selects Model A. This is the first broken link.
  4. Accurate execution of Model A does not rescue the answer because Model A is inapplicable. It earns zero execution points under the declared rubric, so 25+25+0+0=5025+25+0+0=50 points. The later wrong conclusion follows from the earlier selection error.
  5. The signed calibration gap is 90−50=+4090-50=+40 percentage points. Predicted rubric performance exceeded observed performance on this task.
  6. The next action is a short contrast set in which Alpha and Beta appear in varied wording and the learner must name the decisive condition before choosing a model. Rereading both full procedures would be broader than the evidence requires.
  7. The fresh probe supplies a new pair of conditions after a delay, asks for the condition–model justification before execution, and records confidence only after the response is committed.

Independent check. Imagine Kai executes every Model A step perfectly again while Beta remains present. The answer is still wrong. Imagine instead that Kai selects Model B and then makes an arithmetic slip. The first broken link moves to execution. This counterfactual check confirms that selection, not general execution, is the best current diagnosis.

Interpretation. The completed log should read “selection error; high positive calibration gap on this task; contrast conditions and justify model choice; retest with a varied condition.” It should not read “does not know the chapter” or “is overconfident.”

Self-explanation prompt. Which observation ruled out retrieval as the first broken link, and why must the next probe expose model choice before execution?

Error-log row

Task and conditions Attempt evidence First broken link Calibration Next action Fresh probe
Choose between supplied Models A and B; Beta present; no feedback during attempt Correct condition and rules; Model A selected; its steps executed Selection; execution remains testable after correct selection 90% predicted rubric score, 50% earned, +40 points Contrast Alpha/Beta cases and justify choice New condition wording after delay; justify, execute, then predict the rubric score

The same information can be written as six labelled sentences. Visual alignment is not required.

Source Note
claims claim-monitoring-control, claim-calibration-definition, claim-retrieval-cues, claim-error-log-loop, claim-operational-categories, claim-actionable-feedbacksources source-debruin-monitoring-control, source-chen-retrieval-judgments, source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard

The sources support evidence-linked monitoring, retrieval before feedback, task/process feedback, and a next action. All task rules, names, scores, calculations, and log entries are original fictional material.

scenes.title: Repair the reread-everything response
scenes.contentHtml:
Repair the reread-everything response

Misconception
id misconception-wrong-means-reread

Commit a prediction. Two learners obtain the same incorrect final answer. Learner One states the correct model and plan but substitutes a value incorrectly. Learner Two performs every written step correctly for a model whose stated condition is absent. Should both reread the entire topic before another attempt? Explain before continuing.

Tempting model. “A wrong answer means the content was not learned, so the safest response is to reread everything.”

What the model cannot explain. The final correctness signal is identical, but the intermediate evidence differs. Learner One retrieved and selected the needed model; execution first failed. Learner Two executed a procedure but selected an inapplicable model. The score alone cannot discriminate these records, and undirected rereading does not make either causal hypothesis visible.

Replacement model. Preserve the attempt, locate the first broken link, choose a targeted repair, and request a fresh probe. Learner One should slow and check the critical substitution in a new execution case. Learner Two should contrast the conditions that distinguish the models before executing another case. Either diagnosis remains provisional until the fresh probe.

Recheck. A third learner misreads “increase” as “decrease,” then correctly applies the corresponding procedure. Which link breaks first? Interpretation. The appropriate probe asks the learner to restate the required direction before choosing a model; more execution drills would miss the current evidence.

Confidence does not rescue the score-only model

Suppose Learner Two predicts earning 95% of the declared rubric points before feedback. That creates a large positive confidence–performance gap on this task, but it still does not tell you whether selection failed because the condition was unnoticed, misunderstood, or confused with another condition. Calibration data and operation evidence answer related but different questions.

Suppose Learner One predicts earning 30% of the declared rubric points and then performs strongly on two delayed, varied execution probes. The next action may be confidence feedback plus another later verification, not full content reteaching. A controlled laboratory study suggests that repeated trial-level confidence–accuracy correspondence can change with targeted feedback. Its quadratic and signal-detection measures differ from this lesson's single-task signed gap, so the study does not establish how GG changes, justify a guarantee, or support a personality label.

Source Note
claims claim-calibration-definition, claim-retrieval-cues, claim-error-log-loop, claim-operational-categories, claim-calibration-feedbacksources source-debruin-monitoring-control, source-chen-retrieval-judgments, source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard, source-carpenter-calibration-training

The sources support using predictive performance cues and revisable calibration. The tempting model, contrast cases, and repair sequence are original and are presented as a plausible boundary error rather than a measured prevalence claim.

scenes.title: Reconstruct the decision chain
scenes.contentHtml:
Reconstruct the decision chain

Cover or leave the earlier scenes. Answer each prompt before reading the criterion.

Check
id check-reconstruct-chain
  1. Write the five operations in order.
  2. Define “first broken link” without using a personal label.
  3. A learner predicts earning 70% of the declared rubric points and then earns 45%. Calculate and interpret the signed gap.
  4. State why a targeted action is incomplete without a fresh probe.
  5. If visible work supports either a selection or execution error but does not discriminate them, what should the log record?

Criterion after the attempt

The chain is interpret → retrieve → select → execute → verify. The first broken link is the earliest operation whose required evidence is absent or contradicted in this record. It is a provisional task diagnosis, not a claim about the learner.

The calibration calculation is 70−45=+2570-45=+25 percentage points: confidence exceeded rubric performance on this task. The result does not locate the error or establish a stable trait.

A targeted activity is a control decision; a fresh probe produces the next monitoring evidence. Without the probe, the log records an intention but cannot test whether the proposed repair changed performance. When two categories remain possible, record the boundary explicitly and choose a probe that exposes the missing intermediate step—for example, require the learner to state the model choice before executing it.

If fewer than four answers were defensible, revisit scene-concept and scene-calibration, reconstruct the chain again from memory, and use a new pair of confidence and performance values. If all five were defensible, continue without repeating the page.

Source Note
claims claim-monitoring-control, claim-calibration-definition, claim-error-log-loop, claim-operational-categoriessources source-debruin-monitoring-control, source-content-standard

The monitoring–control relation and evidence-linked feedback are source grounded. The exact retrieval prompts, numbers, and first-broken-link procedure are original.

scenes.title: Fade support across new error records
scenes.contentHtml:
Fade support across new error records

Work in order. Commit each response before reading its feedback. The support decreases from a missing field to independent error-log construction.

Unless a record states another rule, every listed practice-rubric criterion is all-or-none: it earns its full stated weight only when the complete criterion is satisfied and otherwise earns zero. Each reported total below shows or follows that rule.

1. Complete a log entry

Record. The task asks which of two supplied rules applies. Its four-part rubric assigns 25% each for interpretation, retrieval, selection, and execution; verification is not scored in this completion task. Mira restates the condition correctly, writes both rules, and leaves the choice blank. After committing the response, Mira predicts earning 35% of the declared rubric points. Observed rubric performance is 50%: interpretation and retrieval earn 25 points each, while selection and execution earn 0.

Complete: “The earliest unsupported operation is ___ because ___. The signed gap is ___. The next action should ___, followed by ___.”

Feedback after an attempt. Selection is the earliest unsupported operation because the evidence supports interpretation and retrieval but contains no model choice. The gap is 35−50=−1535-50=-15 percentage points. Use a supported condition-to-rule comparison, then a fresh choice with different wording. If you chose retrieval, notice that both rules were produced; if you chose execution, notice that no procedure was selected to execute.

2. Compare two records

Both tasks provide the same model and values. Their five-part rubric assigns 15% to interpretation, 15% to retrieval, 10% to selection, 40% to accurate execution, and 20% to the required range check.

  • Record Ash: the learner interprets the task, retrieves and selects the correct model, substitutes one value into the wrong location, notices that the result violates the supplied range, and predicts earning 55% of the declared rubric points. Observed rubric performance is 60%: 15+15+10+0+2015+15+10+0+20.
  • Record Brook: the learner interprets the task, retrieves and selects the correct model, and executes it accurately but does not perform the required range check; the unverified answer happens to be correct. The learner predicts earning 95% of the declared rubric points. Observed rubric performance is 80%: 15+15+10+40+015+15+10+40+0.

For each record, name the first unsupported operation and choose a next probe. Then interpret the confidence gap without calling either learner generally confident or unconfident.

Feedback after an attempt. Ash first fails at execution; verification is actually evidenced by noticing the range violation. The gap is negative 5 points. A fresh probe should make substitution visible and preserve the range check. Brook first fails at verification; the gap is positive 15 points. A new case should require a written range or boundary check before confidence is recorded. Correct output in Brook does not erase the missing required operation.

3. Resolve uncertainty instead of guessing

Record Cedar. This task uses an all-or-none 100-point rubric for the final answer: a correct answer earns 100%, and an incorrect answer earns 0%. The final answer is wrong, and no intermediate work was preserved. Before feedback, the learner assigned an 80% probability to earning full credit. This is the all-or-none exception from the calibration scene: on this 0/100 rubric, the probability of full credit is expressed as 80 predicted points and compared with the realized binary score of 0 points.

Create the shortest defensible log entry.

Feedback after an attempt. Under the declared all-or-none convention, the gap is 80−0=+8080-0=+80 points, but the error category is unresolved. This subtraction is permitted because the probability of full credit was converted to predicted points on the same 0/100 all-or-none scale as the realized outcome; it would not be valid against a partial-credit score. The record does not distinguish interpretation, retrieval, selection, or execution. The next action is not “reread everything”; it is a think-aloud or written-step probe using a new bounded task, with each operation committed before feedback. The new record can then support a targeted repair.

4. Independent mixed case

Record Delta. A fictional classification task supplies three criteria. The learner restates all three, recalls the category definitions, applies two criteria, omits the third without noticing, and produces the wrong category. After committing the response, the learner predicts earning 65% of the declared rubric points. The analytic rubric assigns 20% to interpretation, 20% to retrieval, 40% to applying all three criteria, and 20% to verification. The 40-point application criterion is all-or-none: all three supplied criteria must be applied to earn its 40 points, so applying only two earns zero for that criterion. No verification is performed. The total is therefore 20+20+0+0=40%20+20+0+0=40\%.

Without category hints:

  1. locate the first broken link;
  2. compute and interpret the gap;
  3. state an alternative explanation that the record does not rule out;
  4. choose one repair; and
  5. specify a delayed, varied probe.

Feedback after an attempt. Interpretation and retrieval are supported. Execution first fails when the third criterion is omitted, although an attentional or representation issue remains an alternative because the record does not show why. Producing a category is the output of applying the supplied criteria; it is not a selection error in this lesson's narrower sense of choosing among available models or procedures before execution. The gap is 65−40=+2565-40=+25 points. A matching action is to externalize and check all supplied criteria on two completion cases, then remove the checklist and attempt a new three-criterion case after a delay. If the learner cannot restate the third criterion then, revise the diagnosis toward retrieval; if the criterion is stated but again not applied, execution remains better supported.

Retry rule

Do not replay the same answer sequence. Invent or request an equivalent case with new surface wording, preserve intermediate steps, record confidence after commitment, and decide whether the fresh evidence confirms or moves the first broken link.

Source Note
claims claim-monitoring-control, claim-calibration-definition, claim-retrieval-cues, claim-error-log-loop, claim-operational-categories, claim-actionable-feedbacksources source-debruin-monitoring-control, source-chen-retrieval-judgments, source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard

The practice progression applies source-grounded retrieval and process feedback. All records, rules, numbers, classifications, actions, and feedback are original fictional material; the operation categories remain a provisional instructional model.

scenes.title: Redesign a score-only weekly review
scenes.contentHtml:
Redesign a score-only weekly review

A study group holds a weekly review. Its current dashboard stores each member's name, total percentage, and a red or green icon. The group responds to every low total by assigning the entire chapter again. Members report confidence only once at the end of the week.

Your task is to redesign the process so it chooses evidence-based next actions without collecting private learner profiles.

New constraints

The redesigned review must:

  1. use anonymous task IDs rather than names;
  2. preserve one attempt before feedback and enough intermediate evidence to locate or delimit the first broken link;
  3. record a predicted rubric percentage and the percentage earned on that same rubric;
  4. distinguish an observed fact from a provisional error hypothesis;
  5. assign a targeted action and a fresh varied probe;
  6. delete or keep local any individual record after its decision is complete; and
  7. work as ordered text without icons, color, pointing, dragging, or a timer.

Required output

Submit:

  • a six-field record template;
  • decision rules for resolved and unresolved classifications;
  • two examples showing how the same total score can produce different actions;
  • a confidence-calibration rule that does not label people;
  • an aggregate planning summary containing only counts of action needs, such as “four selection contrasts needed”; and
  • a fresh-probe schedule linked to the next weekly plan.

The group context changes the problem. You must coordinate privacy, aggregation, access, and action selection; renaming the worked learner is insufficient.

Self-check

Ask whether someone using only your ordered text could determine:

  1. what was observed;
  2. which classification is provisional;
  3. why the action matches the evidence;
  4. how confidence compares with performance; and
  5. what new evidence could reverse the decision.

If any answer depends on a color, total score, identity, or hidden conversation, revise the system.

Source Note
claims claim-monitoring-control, claim-calibration-definition, claim-error-log-loop, claim-operational-categoriessources source-debruin-monitoring-control, source-content-standard

The sources support connecting monitoring evidence to control decisions and preserving accessible, actionable feedback. The dashboard, privacy constraints, aggregation rule, and transfer task are original and are not presented as a validated institutional system.

scenes.title: Keep the log active, local, and revisable
scenes.contentHtml:
Keep the log active, local, and revisable

Reconstruct the whole procedure as four verbs:

monitor → diagnose → act → probe

Monitoring preserves task conditions, the attempt before feedback, confidence, and rubric performance. Diagnosis locates the earliest unsupported operation while recording alternatives. Action targets that operation. A fresh probe tests the repair and updates the record.

Decision summary

Evidence pattern Provisional interpretation Proportionate next action Fresh evidence
Requirement misread before an otherwise coherent response Interpretation first unsupported Restate conditions and contrast near-worded prompts New prompt with altered wording
Relevant rule absent before feedback Retrieval first unsupported Brief explanation or restudy, then unaided reconstruction Delayed recall plus application
Rules available but inapplicable model chosen Selection first unsupported Contrast model conditions and justify choice New discriminating case
Applicable plan chosen but step applied incorrectly Execution first unsupported Complete and then independently perform the critical step New values or representation
Result produced without a required plausibility check Verification first unsupported Practise the specific independent check New case requiring the check
Intermediate evidence absent Category unresolved Expose steps in an accessible written or spoken-to-text probe New record before targeted repair

Read linearly, each row states evidence, hypothesis, action, and fresh probe. The table is a route guide, not an exhaustive taxonomy.

Confidence route

For each matched task, compute predicted rubric percentage minus observed rubric percentage. Describe the direction and size on that task. Look for a pattern across comparable, delayed, or varied probes before changing the confidence routine. If predictions repeatedly exceed performance, require the learner to cite observable response evidence before predicting a score. If predictions repeatedly fall below strong performance, compare the completed response with the rubric and schedule another independent verification. When confidence and performance do not use the same scoring rule, keep them separate. Research using repeated trial-level quadratic and signal-detection measures shows that confidence–accuracy correspondence can respond to targeted feedback, but those measures are not this lesson's GG; no one study or log establishes how the signed gap will change or guarantees general transfer.

Recovery route

  • If you cannot reconstruct the prior durable-performance loop, use the five-step scaffold in scene-diagnostic: name a target, attempt without notes, compare after commitment, repair the earliest unsupported step, and retrieve on a delayed or varied case.
  • If you can compute a gap but cannot classify the error, revisit the five-operation chain and practise locating the earliest supported and unsupported links.
  • If you classify confidently from a final score alone, use the unresolved Cedar case and design a probe that exposes intermediate steps.
  • If your actions remain broad, force a one-sentence link: “Because the record first fails at ___, the next activity will exercise ___, and the fresh probe will show ___.”
  • If a response format caused the apparent failure, change the medium while preserving the reasoning. A barrier in handwriting, visual layout, speech, pointing, or speed is not automatically a content error.

Delayed link to the study plan

Carry forward only the current action, fresh-probe timing, and relevant task ID into topic-orientation-study-plan. The Premed graph makes this lesson a prerequisite for that planning outcome. Reopen the evidence after the fresh probe and revise the plan; do not freeze the original category into a learner profile.

Source Note
claims claim-monitoring-control, claim-calibration-definition, claim-retrieval-cues, claim-error-log-loop, claim-operational-categories, claim-calibration-feedback, claim-curriculum-sequence, claim-actionable-feedbacksources source-debruin-monitoring-control, source-chen-retrieval-judgments, source-authoring-evidence, source-content-standard, source-carpenter-calibration-training, source-premed-graph

The synthesis distinguishes established monitoring/calibration constructs, bounded experimental evidence, project standards, graph sequence, and the author-created operational categories. It does not claim that a fixed log or schedule is universally optimal.

scenes.title: Demonstrate classification, calibration, and action
scenes.contentHtml:
Demonstrate classification, calibration, and action

Complete the four items in assessment.json. On the first attempt, use no notes and commit each response before viewing answer logic. The assessment is untimed and retakeable. An equivalent retake changes the fictional rules, values, surface wording, and error boundary.

Each assessment-rubric criterion is all-or-none: award its full maxPoints only when every requirement in that criterion description is satisfied; otherwise award zero for that criterion. This rule makes the item totals reproducible while still separating the four dimensions in each 12-point open response.

Item map

  1. item-classify-selection asks you to locate a first broken link from visible intermediate work.
  2. item-calculate-gap asks you to calculate and interpret a signed confidence–performance gap.
  3. item-build-error-log asks you to complete the full evidence → diagnosis → action → probe chain across two contrasting records.
  4. item-transfer-review-system asks you to redesign a score-only weekly review under privacy and access constraints.

Mastery evidence

A mastery-ready response must:

  • distinguish observations from a provisional category;
  • locate the earliest unsupported operation or record a genuine unresolved boundary;
  • interpret confidence against performance on the same declared scale;
  • choose an action that directly exercises the proposed broken link;
  • specify new evidence that could confirm or revise the classification; and
  • preserve an equivalent untimed text route without personal data.

Correct category words without evidence do not satisfy the analytic rubrics. A calculation without task-bounded interpretation is incomplete. A proposed action without a fresh probe cannot show whether repair occurred.

Feedback and retry

Use each item's commonErrors to identify the first failing distinction: score versus process evidence, confidence versus performance, category versus trait, broad restudy versus targeted action, or activity versus fresh probe. Return to the matching scene, construct a new fictional record that crosses that boundary, and retry. Do not memorize option IDs or lower the reasoning standard.

Callout

Assessment records are educational only. Use fictional or local self-study data. Do not submit a real person's identifiable grades, health information, disability details, admissions record, or clinical performance.

Source Note
claims claim-monitoring-control, claim-calibration-definition, claim-error-log-loop, claim-operational-categories, claim-actionable-feedbacksources source-debruin-monitoring-control, source-content-standard, source-authoring-evidence

The assessment aligns with the source-grounded monitoring–control and actionable-feedback principles while evaluating the lesson's explicitly provisional operational model. All stimuli and answer logic are original and remain pending independent review.

aiAssistance.disclosure: Material author-revision assistance was provided by Codex CLI (OpenAI gpt-5.6-sol), Codex CLI 0.144.6, isolated author run codex-cli/7d0c9d38-d123-40d4-8f94-138ca26ca4da/author-revision-prem-lpp-004-v012. The recorded SHA-256 digest covers the complete nonempty author-revision instruction file supplied to this run; hidden provider/system instructions are excluded. Accountable principal: github:VSBDev.

items.prompt: A task supplies two model rules. The learner restates the task condition accurately, recalls both rules, chooses the model whose condition is absent, and executes that model without a procedural slip. Which entry best identifies the first broken link and the next probe?

options.text: Retrieval error; reconstruct both rules from memory in a new case before choosing and executing a model.
Selection error; contrast the models' conditions, then justify a model choice in a new case before execution.
Execution error; select a model in a varied case, then expose each execution step and verify the result.
Interpretation error; restate a newly worded condition before choosing and executing a model.

answer.summary: The first contradicted operation is model selection, so the next evidence should expose model choice.
answer.reasoning: Interpretation and retrieval are supported by the accurate condition and rules. Execution of an inapplicable model can be internally correct while the answer remains wrong; selection therefore breaks first. Contrasting the applicability conditions and requiring a justified choice in a fresh case directly tests the diagnosis.
acceptableResponses: Selection error followed by a new discriminating model-choice probe
commonErrors: Calling the error retrieval ignores the accurately recalled rules.
Calling it execution confuses correct performance of the wrong procedure with choosing an applicable procedure.
Calling it interpretation ignores the accurate restatement of the task condition.

items.prompt: After committing a response, a learner predicts earning 75% of the points on the declared analytic rubric and then earns 40%. Calculate predicted rubric percentage minus observed rubric percentage. Enter the signed gap in percentage points; then compare your interpretation with the answer reasoning.

answer.summary: The signed calibration gap is positive 35 percentage points.
answer.reasoning: Predicted rubric percentage minus observed rubric percentage is 75 minus 40, which equals positive 35 percentage points. The prediction exceeded observed rubric performance on this task. The result neither locates the error nor establishes an overconfident personality; those require process evidence and a pattern across comparable probes.
acceptableResponses: 35 percentage points, with confidence above performance on this task
commonErrors: Negative 35 reverses the declared confidence-minus-performance convention.
Calling the learner overconfident converts one task result into an unsupported trait label.
Using 75% as the performance estimate discards the rubric evidence.

items.prompt: For Records A and B, create concise error-log entries. Separate observations from the provisional category, identify the first broken link, calculate confidence minus performance, name an unresolved alternative where appropriate, choose a matching action, and specify a delayed or varied fresh probe.
items.stimulus: Both fictional tasks state that condition Gamma is present and supply two model rules. Model G applies under Gamma; Model H applies under Eta. The rubric awards 20% each for interpretation, retrieval, and selection, 30% for execution, and 10% for verification. Each criterion is all-or-none: it earns its full weight only when its complete description is satisfied and otherwise earns zero. Execution credit is also contingent on selecting a model that is applicable under the stated condition and then executing that applicable model accurately; accurate execution of an inapplicable model earns zero execution credit. Record A: the learner restates Gamma, recalls both rules, selects Model G, transposes two supplied values during execution, performs no check, predicts earning 70% of the rubric points, and earns 60% (20 + 20 + 20 + 0 + 0). Record B: the learner restates Gamma, recalls both rules, selects Model H, executes Model H's supplied steps accurately, performs no check, predicts earning 50% of the rubric points, and earns 40% (20 + 20 + 0 + 0 + 0).

answer.correct: A rubric-scored response that classifies Record A provisionally as execution first unsupported with a +10-point gap and Record B as selection first unsupported with a +10-point gap; distinguishes observations from hypotheses; records uncertainty; assigns a targeted repair to each; and specifies fresh probes that can revise the classifications.
answer.summary: Identical signed gaps do not imply identical errors or actions.
answer.reasoning: In A, interpretation, retrieval, and selection earn 20 points each. Execution earns 0 because of the transposition, and verification earns 0 because no check was performed, so the observed score is 60%. Execution first fails; missing verification is later and may have allowed the error to persist. A targeted action exposes the substitution step, preserves a check, and retests with new values or representation. In B, interpretation and retrieval earn 20 points each, but Model H is inapplicable under Gamma, so selection earns 0. Under the declared contingent scoring rule, accurately carrying out inapplicable Model H earns 0 execution points; no check earns 0 verification points, so the observed score is 40%. Selection first fails. A targeted action contrasts Gamma and Eta and requires a justified choice in a new case. Both gaps are 70 minus 60 and 50 minus 40, or positive 10 points, but calibration direction alone does not locate the different errors.
acceptableResponses: Any two auditable entries with the same supported classifications, calculations, targeted actions, fresh probes, and explicit provisional scope.
For A, an answer may record verification as a secondary error if execution remains the first broken link.
For B, an answer may request a prompt-restatement probe if it explains why interpretation still remains an alternative despite the current accurate restatement.
commonErrors: Assigning both records the same category from their equal calibration gaps confuses calibration with diagnosis.
Calling A a selection error ignores the stated correct model choice.
Calling B an execution error ignores the accurate execution of an inapplicable model.
Assigning full-topic rereading to both records fails to match action to evidence.
Omitting a fresh probe leaves the provisional diagnosis untested.

items.prompt: Redesign a weekly study-group dashboard that currently stores names and total scores, labels every low score red, and assigns the entire chapter again. Produce a privacy-preserving, non-color-dependent process that supports classification, calibration, targeted action, and revisable fresh probes.
items.stimulus: The group needs an aggregate plan for the next week but does not need individual identities, health information, disability details, or permanent learner profiles. The process must work as ordered text and remain untimed.

answer.correct: A rubric-scored redesign using anonymous task-level records, attempts and intermediate evidence before feedback, confidence and rubric performance on matched scales, provisional first-broken-link classifications or unresolved boundaries, matching actions, varied or delayed fresh probes, aggregate action counts, local/deleted individual records, and an equivalent untimed ordered-text route.
answer.summary: Replace score-only labels with an evidence-to-action system while minimizing personal data.
answer.reasoning: A total score cannot locate the earliest unsupported operation, and color or identity adds no necessary decision evidence. Anonymous task records preserve observations and calibration; provisional categories drive targeted actions; fresh probes test the decisions. Aggregating only counts of action needs supports planning without turning task evidence into a lasting personal profile. Ordered text preserves the same operation without color, pointer input, or time pressure.
acceptableResponses: Any concise system that preserves the same monitoring, provisional diagnosis, calibration, targeted control, fresh-probe, privacy, and accessibility operations.
The response may keep individual records locally rather than delete them if it states a bounded purpose and does not share identifying details.
commonErrors: Replacing red and green with different colors still makes the distinction color dependent.
Keeping only total scores leaves error classification unsupported.
Storing names or personal characteristics expands the data beyond the stated planning need.
Assigning an activity without a fresh probe prevents revision from later evidence.
A drag-only or timed interface changes access without serving the target construct.

criteria.description: All-or-none: identifies selection as the first contradicted operation and chooses a new probe that exposes applicability and model choice before execution.
All-or-none: calculates positive 35 percentage points and interprets it as confidence exceeding rubric performance on this task without a trait inference.
All-or-none: separates observations from hypotheses and identifies execution first for A and selection first for B with a defensible uncertainty statement.
All-or-none: calculates and task-bounds both positive 10-point gaps without using them to infer the error category or a personal trait.
All-or-none: chooses an execution-focused action for A and a model-condition contrast for B, explaining how each matches the first broken link.
All-or-none: specifies varied or delayed probes that expose the relevant operation and could confirm or revise each classification.
All-or-none: captures anonymous task conditions, committed attempts, intermediate evidence, matched confidence, and rubric performance rather than only totals.
All-or-none: uses provisional classifications or explicit unresolved boundaries to select matching actions and fresh probes.
All-or-none: minimizes personal data and creates an aggregate plan from action-need counts without permanent learner labels.
All-or-none: provides an untimed ordered-text route whose meaning and operation do not depend on color, layout, pointing, dragging, or a sensory channel.

claims.statement: Metacognitive monitoring evaluates evidence about current learning or performance, while metacognitive control uses that judgment to regulate a next learning or performance action; new action results can update later monitoring.
claims.scope: A metacognitive framework used for bounded academic study and performance decisions, with particular relevance to medical education in the cited review.
claims.uncertainty: Monitoring and control are broad construct families; the framework does not validate this lesson's exact log fields or guarantee improved learning.

claims.statement: Calibration concerns the relation between a confidence judgment and observed performance on a defined task; a signed predicted-minus-observed rubric gap is interpretable only when both values use the same declared scoring rule and scale, and it describes one task rather than a stable learner trait.
claims.scope: Task-bounded comparison where confidence predicts the percentage of points on the same declared 0-100 rubric used to score observed performance.
claims.uncertainty: A single gap can be affected by rubric quality, scale use, task sampling, support, and timing; it does not estimate a stable personal disposition.

claims.statement: In the cited laboratory experiments, judgments made after retrieval trials corresponded more closely with later accuracy than judgments made after restudy, supporting retrieval performance as one useful monitoring cue within the studied tasks.
claims.scope: Bounded memory tasks and broader evidence on retrieval before answer reveal; applied here as a study-design cue rather than a universal measurement rule.
claims.uncertainty: The cited correspondence is an across-item rank relation between judgments and later accuracy, not this lesson's single-task signed predicted-minus-observed gap. The effect and useful cues can differ by task, knowledge type, delay, feedback, learner, and final assessment; retrieval is not asserted to be the only valid cue.

claims.statement: A decision-oriented error log can be modelled as observation, provisional diagnosis, matching action, and fresh probe, so that later performance can confirm or revise the earlier hypothesis.
claims.scope: An author-created educational procedure for low-stakes, bounded study records where intermediate work and follow-up probes are available.
claims.uncertainty: This exact log format has not been validated as an intervention, and completing it does not by itself demonstrate learning, retention, or transfer.

claims.statement: For the purpose of selecting a next probe, a response can be inspected in the order interpret, retrieve, select, execute, and verify, with the earliest unsupported operation recorded provisionally rather than treated as an exhaustive cause or learner label.
claims.scope: A non-exhaustive author-created operation chain for the fictional and self-study tasks in this lesson.
claims.uncertainty: Real errors may involve interacting causes, missing evidence, task design, access barriers, affect, or knowledge not represented by this chain; independent review must assess its instructional adequacy.

claims.statement: In one controlled laboratory study, adaptive feedback about repeated trial-level metacognitive judgments improved calibration assessed with a quadratic score equal to one minus the Brier score on a trained perceptual task relative to performance-only feedback and generalized to an untrained recognition-memory task.
claims.scope: The cited study's small experimental groups, adaptive training, repeated trial-level confidence judgments, quadratic score equal to one minus the Brier score, signal-detection indices, perceptual discrimination task, and recognition-memory transfer task.
claims.uncertainty: The study did not use this lesson's single-task signed predicted-minus-observed rubric gap and does not establish how that gap behaves, universal transfer, effects in Premed learning, or the effectiveness of this lesson's error log.

claims.statement: The Premed graph places metacognition and error logs after mastery, retrieval, and spacing and before study-plan construction, making prior performance evidence an input to a later plan.
claims.scope: Current EmbeddedKnowledge curriculum identity and prerequisite relationships for PREM-01.03 through PREM-01.05.
claims.uncertainty: The graph order is a curriculum design decision, not empirical proof of one universally optimal individual sequence or schedule.

claims.statement: Useful feedback after a genuine attempt identifies what was correct or incorrect, explains the relevant process or principle, and gives an actionable next step rather than only a score or personal judgment.
claims.scope: Educational feedback for the bounded conceptual and procedural tasks used in this lesson.
claims.uncertainty: Feedback effects are heterogeneous and depend on content, timing, prior knowledge, task design, and whether the learner performs the intended operation.

terms.definition: In this lesson, collecting and interpreting evidence about current knowledge or task performance, including the conditions, response, score, and confidence judgment.
In this lesson, selecting or changing a learning action in response to a monitoring judgment and then collecting new evidence from the result.

In this lesson, a task-bounded prediction made after committing a response but before feedback of the percentage of rubric points the response will earn; for an all-or-none 0/100 rubric, a probability of full credit can be expressed as predicted points on that same scale.

In this lesson, the relation between predicted and observed rubric points for one defined task using the same declared 0-100 scoring rule, including the explicitly marked probability-to-points convention for an all-or-none 0/100 rubric; research may operationalize calibration with different measures over repeated judgments.
Predicted rubric percentage minus observed rubric percentage for one matched task; a positive value means the prediction exceeded performance and a negative value means the prediction was lower.
The earliest operation in the lesson's interpret–retrieve–select–execute–verify chain whose required evidence is absent or contradicted in the current task record.
A provisional action-oriented label for the first unsupported operation in a bounded task record, not an exhaustive cause or a label for the learner.
A structured record linking task conditions and attempt evidence to a provisional error hypothesis, confidence calibration, a targeted learning action, and a fresh probe.
A study activity selected because it directly exercises the operation currently proposed as the first broken link.
A new attempt, usually varied or delayed, designed to reveal whether a targeted repair changed the relevant operation and whether the earlier diagnosis should be retained or revised.

attributionHtml:
Attribution and provenance

Lesson contribution

  • Lesson: PREM-LPP-004 — Use metacognition and error logs to choose the next learning action
  • Version: 0.1.2
  • Accountable contributor: VSBDev (github:VSBDev), verified through the authenticated GitHub CLI on 2026-07-20.
  • Content license: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC-BY-4.0).
  • Suggested attribution: Based on EmbeddedKnowledge, “PREM-LPP-004 — Use metacognition and error logs to choose the next learning action,” version 0.1.2, by VSBDev, licensed CC BY 4.0. Changes: describe any changes.

The contributor attests that the original lesson prose, fictional rules, examples, tables, calculations, prompts, feedback, assessment items, rubrics, and authoring brief may be licensed on those terms.

Material agent assistance

  • System: Codex CLI
  • Provider: OpenAI
  • Model: gpt-5.6-sol
  • Version: Codex CLI 0.144.6
  • Run ID: codex-cli/7d0c9d38-d123-40d4-8f94-138ca26ca4da/author-revision-prem-lpp-004-v012
  • Material-instructions digest: sha256:3a643c1ce1be8f63ff5fe17b812b63cc03687e34b9c454d54f7be8fec9e7eeb0
  • Digest scope: SHA-256 of the complete nonempty author-revision instruction file supplied to this isolated run. Hidden provider/system instructions that cannot be exported are excluded.
  • Role boundary: author only. This run did not review, approve, adjudicate, publish, push, or merge the lesson.

Source use and expression

The records in references.json were used only for factual reference and project-standard/curriculum identity. The lesson independently synthesizes those facts in an original instructional structure and original language.

No source wording, quotation, figure, table, dataset, experimental stimulus, worked example, assessment item, rubric, media, or distinctive source organization was copied, translated, adapted, redistributed, or lightly redrawn. The five-operation chain and six-field error log are explicit author inferences and are not represented as validated universal taxonomies.

The three external scholarly sources were accessed through NCBI/PubMed Central after checking NCBI's policies and usage guidance. Their PMC open-access records display CC BY, but this lesson nevertheless relies on the conservative facts-only/original-expression basis. Exact academic support, scope, uncertainty, rights evidence, and dated agent-access status are recorded in references.json.

Repository sources are within the CC BY 4.0 content scope declared in CONTENT-LICENSE.md. Citation supports claims but does not transfer the rights to source expression.

Third-party assets and data

No third-party image, diagram, audio, video, code, dataset, patient material, assessment bank, or other asset is included. lesson.json.thirdPartyAssets is empty. All names and task records are fictional and do not describe real learners.

Limitations and review state

This is a draft authoring candidate. All claims and source confidence remain pending-review. Automated validation and author self-audit do not constitute independent academic, learning-design, accessibility-and-rights review, learner testing, adjudication, publication, course credit, clinical competence, or professional authorization.
